iterations.strategy: Root cause: main_scan was dominated by unnecessary per-row anti-join lookup work before exploiting the highly selective date window; this is primarily a work-elimination issue (Category C) with plan restructuring. The revised plan performs zonemap pruning and predicate-only scanning first to materialize candidate rowids, then applies anti-join only to candidates with a cache-resident bloom prefilter before exact index lookup, and aggregates only surviving rows. This reduces random pre-index probes and late-loads join/group columns only when needed.

Root cause: most hot time comes from touching wide num columns and initializing anti-join resources before establishing whether the 2023 ddate window yields any rows; this is a Category C work-elimination fix with plan restructuring. The plan now makes ddate the first-stage scan key, materializes candidate rowids, applies uom/value only on candidates, and defers loading/using pre anti-join structures and join key columns unless survivors exist, eliminating unnecessary random lookups and reducing loaded bytes on likely-empty windows.

Root cause: remaining hot-path cost is dominated by startup work that is not proportional to output, especially unconditional mmap/prefetch and dictionary loading before confirming any qualifying 2023 rows. Strategy category C (work elimination) with plan restructuring: first read only the ddate zonemap to determine overlap, immediately return empty output when no blocks overlap, defer loading all large columns, anti-join index, and tag/version dictionaries until survivors exist, and avoid whole-column prefetch in hot runs so memory traffic is paid only for blocks and rows that can contribute to final groups.

Root cause: hot time is dominated by main_scan doing row-wise predicate evaluation across most of num because ddate zonemap pruning is coarse, so work is not proportional to rows reaching anti-join/output. Strategy category C (work elimination) with plan restructuring: drive the scan from num_uom_hash posting rowids for USD, prune those rowids by zonemap candidate blocks before touching ddate/value, and fuse ddate+value filtering with anti-join probe and aggregation in one pass to remove intermediate survivor materialization and reduce memory traffic and scheduling overhead.

Root cause is a storage-level pruning ceiling: the existing ddate zonemap block granularity is too coarse, so many mixed-date blocks still force row-wise ddate checks in main_scan. Strategy category E (storage extension): build and register a reusable fine-grained num.ddate zonemap at 4096-row blocks, use it as the first pruning structure with the same deferred-load anti-join pipeline from iter 3, and keep pre index and join-key column loading conditional on survivor presence so scan work is reduced without reintroducing heavy posting-index setup overhead.
